title: CVE-2026-41679 (CVSS 9.8): Paperclip AI Orchestration Platform Unauthenticated RCE via Six-Step Authorization Bypass Chain
coverage_detail: Policy vectimus-supchain-010 provides partial coverage: it blocks an AI agent from installing unverified versions of the paperclipai or @paperclipai/server npm packages via shell_command or package_operation tool calls, preventing an agent from introducing the vulnerable package into a project. However, the primary exploit chain — an external attacker chaining six unauthenticated HTTP API calls against a running Paperclip server (open self-registration, CLI challenge self-approval flaw, missing import endpoint auth check, and unsandboxed bash execution) — targets the agent orchestration platform's HTTP control plane directly and is outside Vectimus's tool-call enforcement boundary. Vectimus cannot intercept HTTP requests from an external attacker to a deployed Paperclip server instance.
gap_description: The six-step RCE exploit chain operates entirely within the Paperclip server's HTTP/API surface — an unauthenticated network-layer attack that predates any agent tool call. Vectimus hooks fire only at tool-call time within a developer's agent session; they cannot intercept external attacker HTTP traffic to a running orchestration server. Additionally, vectimus-supchain-010 only blocks agent-initiated npm install tool calls; installations performed manually by developers, in CI/CD pipelines, or before Vectimus was deployed are not covered. The secondary cross-tenant token minting flaw (GHSA-47wq-cj9q-wpmp) is similarly a server-side authentication defect outside the tool-call boundary.

sources.title: Paperclip AI Vulnerability: RCE and Cross-Tenant Takeover
Paperclip RCE: Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution via Six API Calls
Paperclip Server RCE via Agent Privilege Escalation
PT-2026-34622: Paperclip AI Agent Orchestration Platform RCE

summary: CVE-2026-41679 (CVSS 9.8) is a critical unauthenticated RCE vulnerability in Paperclip, an open-source AI agent orchestration platform distributed as an npm package. An unauthenticated attacker chains four authorization failures — open self-registration, a CLI challenge self-approval flaw, a missing import endpoint auth check, and unsandboxed bash command execution — into a six-API-call sequence taking approximately 30 seconds to achieve full filesystem access, complete database read/write, and cross-tenant data exposure across all @paperclipai/server and paperclipai versions below 2026.416.0. A post-patch cross-tenant token minting flaw (GHSA-47wq-cj9q-wpmp) was additionally disclosed. The exploit chain is a server-side HTTP attack against the agent orchestration platform's control plane, which is outside Vectimus's tool-call enforcement boundary. Vectimus's supply chain policy vectimus-supchain-010 partially mitigates risk by blocking agent-initiated npm installs of unverified package versions, but cannot intercept an external attacker's HTTP requests to a deployed Paperclip server.

title: TeamPCP Xinference PyPI Compromise: Trojanised AI Inference Framework Versions Steal Cloud Credentials, SSH Keys and Crypto Wallets from LLM Infrastructure
coverage_detail: No existing Vectimus policy blocks pip installation of the compromised xinference versions (2.6.0, 2.6.1, 2.6.2). Policy vectimus-supchain-002 only blocks pip installs from non-standard indexes, which does not apply here because PyPI is the standard index. Policy vectimus-supchain-010 provides npm hash-allowlist verification but has no pip equivalent. Policy SUPPLY-001 demonstrates the version-denylist pattern for langchain-core (CVE-2025-68664) but covers a different package. A new supchain policy is required to block agent-initiated installation of the known-compromised xinference 2.6.x versions.
gap_description: No pip hash-verification or version-denylist policy exists for the compromised xinference 2.6.x range. When an AI agent (Claude Code, Cursor, GitHub Copilot) executes `pip install xinference` or explicitly pins versions 2.6.0–2.6.2 via a shell_command or package_operation, Vectimus has no rule to intercept the request. The npm hash-allowlist pattern (vectimus-supchain-010) and the version-denylist pattern (SUPPLY-001) both demonstrate viable approaches; neither has been applied to pip/PyPI for this package.

sources.title: Xinference PyPI Compromise — JFrog Security Research
Xinference Allegedly Hacked by TeamPCP — Malicious Package in PyPI (OX Security)
Three Supply Chain Campaigns Hit npm, PyPI and Docker Hub in 48 Hours (GitGuardian)

summary: Three consecutive releases of the Xinference AI inference framework on PyPI (versions 2.6.0, 2.6.1, 2.6.2) were found to contain a credential-stealing payload attributed to the TeamPCP threat actor. A base64-encoded implant injected into xinference/__init__.py executed on import, harvesting AWS credentials, Google Cloud configs, Kubernetes tokens, SSH keys, environment variables and crypto wallets before exfiltrating them to a C2 server at whereisitat[.]lucyatemysuperbox[.]space. Xinference has over 600,000 total downloads and is widely used to self-host LLMs and embedding models, making it a high-value target in AI developer supply chains. An AI agent setting up or updating an LLM inference stack is a plausible direct installation vector. Vectimus currently lacks a pip equivalent of the npm hash-allowlist policy (vectimus-supchain-010), and no version-denylist rule covers xinference 2.6.x analogous to SUPPLY-001's coverage of langchain-core CVE-2025-68664. Maintainers yanked the three compromised versions; researchers note contested attribution with TeamPCP denying involvement via social media.
recommended_policy_description: Add two complementary policies to the `supchain` pack. (1) An immediate version-denylist rule (modelled on SUPPLY-001 for langchain-core) that forbids `package_operation` and `shell_command` actions whose command string matches `*xinference==2.6.0*`, `*xinference==2.6.1*`, or `*xinference==2.6.2*`, with a suggested alternative to pin to the next clean release. (2) A broader pip hash-allowlist policy (modelled on `vectimus-supchain-010` for npm) that blocks any `package_operation` or `shell_command` pip install where `context.pip_package_verified` is not set to true by the Vectimus supply-chain checker, requiring operator-maintained hash registration in `.vectimus/supply-allowlist.toml` before an AI agent may install a Python package. Policy (1) delivers immediate protection for this campaign; policy (2) closes the structural gap that allowed TeamPCP-style PyPI compromises to go undetected at the tool-call boundary.
